name: Vijnana-Bhairava-Tantra (position-entry)
dates_notes: This is a position-entry, not an author-entry. The Vijñāna-Bhairava-Tantra is anonymous and is internally datable to the eighth–ninth centuries CE on the basis of its citation by Kṣemarāja, Śivopādhyāya (the eighteenth-century commentator), and references in the Tantrāloka-tradition. Modern scholarship — Singh's translation, Padoux, Silburn — accepts the eighth–ninth-century placement.

lineage_polemical.note: Kṣemarāja's commentary (the *Uddyota* or *Vivṛti*) on the Vijñāna-Bhairava-Tantra is the principal Kashmirian interpretive vehicle. The relation is engaged-from-within: Kṣemarāja is doing the non-dual Śaiva philosophy of the source-tantra's 112 *dhāraṇās*.

Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* draws on the Vijñāna-Bhairava-Tantra as one of the principal source-tantras for the Trika's analysis of *upāya* (means) and *dhāraṇā* (focused awareness); the 112 *dhāraṇās* of the Vijñāna-Bhairava are integrated into Abhinavagupta's analysis of the *śākta-upāya* (the Śakti-means).

core_thesis: The *Vijñāna-Bhairava-Tantra* is the central Trika dhāraṇā-tantra — the manual of 112 *dhāraṇās* (focused awareness practices, contemplative techniques) by which the practitioner directly encounters Bhairava-consciousness as one's own nature. The work is anonymous, comparatively short (162 verses on the standard reckoning), presenting itself as a dialogue between the goddess (Bhairavī) and Bhairava in which the goddess asks for the supreme teaching and Bhairava responds with the 112 techniques. The techniques are organized not as a sequential path but as 112 equivalent doorways — each *dhāraṇā* is a sufficient means to recognize Bhairava-consciousness, and any one suffices for the practitioner whose disposition fits it. The techniques range across breath (*prāṇa*-techniques: focusing awareness in the pause between in-breath and out-breath; following the breath into the *dvādaśānta* twelve-fingers above the head), sense-perception (*indriya*-techniques: dissolving into the experience of music, taste, or visual beauty), emotion (*bhāva*-techniques: focusing awareness in the moment of intense emotion, the moment of yawning, the moment of sneezing), thought (*saṃkalpa*-techniques: focusing in the gap between thoughts, the moment of being startled), and *void*-contemplation (*śūnya*-techniques: contemplating the void of space, of consciousness, of identity). The unifying point is that any focused awareness, sustained, opens onto Bhairava-consciousness; the tantra's framework is non-prescriptive variety rather than ritual hierarchy. The work is central to the Trika as the principal manual of the *śākta-upāya* and is the document through which the school's contemplative programme is articulated for the practitioner who is past the ritual stage. Kṣemarāja's commentary (the *Uddyota* — its precise authorship is debated; some manuscripts attribute the commentary to Śivopādhyāya, an eighteenth-century commentator) is the principal interpretive vehicle.

engaged_works.title: Vijnana-Bhairava-Tantra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Anonymous; dated by its citation chain in Kashmirian non-dual Śaiva commentarial literature and by the developed character of its dhāraṇā apparatus.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Anonymous; ascribed by tradition to Bhairava revelation. The text presents itself as a dialogue between the goddess and Bhairava.

ascription_evidence.description: Singh's translation and commentary (1979); Silburn's French translation (1961); Padoux; standard reference in Trika scholarship.

engaged_works.summary: 162 verses in dialogue between the goddess Bhairavī and Bhairava. The opening verses (1–22) establish the frame: the goddess asks for the supreme nature of Bhairava-consciousness, and Bhairava responds that his supreme nature is beyond all attributes and all instruments of cognition and is to be encountered directly through *dhāraṇā* (focused awareness). The body of the text (verses 23–138) gives the 112 *dhāraṇās* — breath-based, sense-based, emotion-based, thought-based, and void-based techniques, each a sufficient doorway to Bhairava-consciousness. The closing verses (139–162) treat the *jīvanmukti* (liberation-while-living) of the practitioner who has realized Bhairava-consciousness and the universal character of that realization (the realized practitioner sees all things as Bhairava). The work is one of the principal Trika source-tantras and is the canonical Sanskrit text of the developed *śākta-upāya* contemplative programme.

reception_notes: The Vijñāna-Bhairava-Tantra is the principal Trika dhāraṇā-tantra and the document through which the school's contemplative programme is articulated for the practitioner past the ritual stage. The Kashmirian commentary tradition (Kṣemarāja; the *Uddyota* whose authorship is debated) is the principal interpretive vehicle. Modern scholarship — Singh's English translation (1979), Silburn's French translation (1961), Padoux — has established the tantra as one of the most accessible and most frequently cited Trika sources for the modern reader. **None of these scholars' works is on disk in our corpus; all cited via field-standard summary.**
